medical__special_needs_information.physical_conditions:
Ethan is generally healthy and active with no diagnosed chronic physical conditions. He wears prescription eyeglasses for mild nearsightedness (distance vision) and should have them on during any activities that involve reading from the board, watching videos, or playing ball games where depth perception matters. His current glasses have dark blue frames.

He has a history of mild seasonal allergies (mainly in the spring and early fall), which sometimes cause sneezing, a runny nose, and itchy eyes when pollen counts are high. These symptoms are usually well-controlled with over-the-counter medication taken at home before school, but staff should be aware that he may occasionally need a tissue or a brief moment to wash his face and hands if he has been outside for an extended time.

He does not have asthma, seizures, diabetes, or any known heart conditions. He has full range of motion and can climb, run, and participate in sports and playground activities without difficulty. If he ever complains of feeling lightheaded or unusually tired during vigorous play, giving him a short water break and a chance to sit down usually helps.
medical__special_needs_information.special_attention_medications_routines:
Ethan takes a daily children’s multivitamin and a chewable allergy tablet (cetirizine 5 mg) at home in the morning during peak allergy seasons (roughly March–June and late August–October). He does not need any medication administered during the after-school program under normal circumstances.

He has a fairly regular routine that helps him feel comfortable and settled:
- After school, he usually has a small snack and water first. Having a snack within the first 30 minutes helps prevent him from getting cranky or having a mild headache.
- After snack, he does better if he can have 10–15 minutes of quiet or semi-quiet activity before very vigorous play (e.g., reading, drawing, or building with blocks). This seems to help him transition from the school day to more active play.
- He sometimes gets a bit overwhelmed in very noisy, crowded indoor settings. In those moments, offering him the option to move to a quieter activity table or a calmer corner of the room for a short period (5–10 minutes) usually helps him reset.

He does not currently have an IEP or 504 plan and is not on any behavioral or psychiatric medications. If any medication ever becomes necessary during program hours, we will provide updated written instructions and a doctor’s note as required.
medical__special_needs_information.able_to_participate_extended_care:
yes – Ethan is able to participate fully in the extended care and after-school program, including both indoor and outdoor activities and the use of climbing equipment.

The only minor limitations or considerations are:
1) Seasonal Allergies: On high-pollen days, he may prefer slightly shorter intervals of outdoor time or may need to wear a light baseball cap to keep pollen out of his hair and eyes. If his eyes look red or he starts sneezing a lot, allowing him to wash his hands and face and giving him a brief rest indoors is usually enough.
2) Glasses: He should wear his glasses for all activities where good distance vision is needed (sports, group games, or watching any demonstrations). Please encourage him to put them in his case and store them in his backpack if he takes them off for rough play so they don’t get lost or broken.

Otherwise, Ethan can run, jump, climb, and participate in structured games, arts and crafts, and all typical program components without restrictions. If there are any new concerns observed by staff (e.g., frequent complaints of being tired, headaches, or difficulty keeping up physically), we would appreciate a note or quick conversation so we can follow up with his pediatrician if needed.
medical__special_needs_information.restricted_foods:
Ethan does not have any diagnosed food allergies. However, there are a few preferences and mild sensitivities to be aware of:

- Tree Nuts & Peanuts: No known allergy, but we prefer that he not share food with other children, especially items containing peanuts or tree nuts, due to general safety and the possibility of other children’s allergies. He may eat snacks provided by the program that are peanut/tree-nut safe per YMCA policy.
- Dairy: He can have dairy, but large amounts of straight milk on an empty stomach sometimes upset his stomach. Yogurt and cheese are usually fine. If milk is offered, it’s best if he has it with a solid snack.
- Spicy Foods: Spicy or heavily seasoned snacks tend to give him a mild stomachache. Please avoid offering him very spicy chips or similar snacks.

If any concerns arise about possible reactions to foods, please let us know so we can follow up with his pediatrician or an allergist if needed.
medical__special_needs_information.special_concerns_information:
Ethan is an energetic, friendly, and talkative 9-year-old who generally adjusts well to new environments once he feels comfortable with the routine and staff. He is in 3rd grade and enjoys reading graphic novels, building with LEGO, drawing, and playing soccer. He tends to do best when expectations are clear and consistent.

Behavior and Social Interaction:
- Ethan is usually very cooperative and eager to help staff with small tasks (passing out papers, helping clean up, etc.). Being given small responsibilities can be very motivating for him.
- In group settings, he sometimes talks out of turn when he gets excited about an activity or wants to share an idea. Gentle reminders like, “Let’s raise our hand first,” or “We’ll come back to you after Sarah finishes,” work well.
- He is sensitive to being corrected in front of peers. If he is having trouble following directions, a quiet, calm reminder given to him one-on-one usually leads to a quick improvement.

Emotional/Behavioral Notes:
- When he is tired or hungry, he may become a bit whiny or overly silly. Offering a snack, a drink of water, and a short break generally helps reset his mood.
- If he feels left out of a game or activity, he might withdraw and sit by himself rather than saying something. Staff can help by gently inviting him to join another group or pairing him with one or two kind peers.
- He responds very well to positive reinforcement—specific praise such as, “Thank you for listening the first time,” or “I like how you helped clean up the blocks,” really encourages him.

Communication with Family:
- Both parents work full-time but are reachable by phone and email. If there is any behavioral incident (e.g., conflict with another child, repeated difficulty following rules, or any injury beyond a minor scrape), we would appreciate a brief summary at pick-up or a quick phone call/email.

Safety:
- Ethan knows his parents’ names and cell phone numbers but can get flustered if asked on the spot. In an emergency, he may need a moment to recall details, so having the information clearly documented on file is important.

Overall, Ethan is a good-natured child who thrives in structured environments with clear rules, predictable routines, and warm, encouraging adults. Please feel free to contact us if you notice anything out of the ordinary—changes in mood, energy level, or social behavior—so we can work together to support him.
